ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for wild rice as a food item; not a clinical substance requiring SNOMED coding.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dry serving of uncooked wild rice is approximately 45 g (about 3 tablespoons), which expands to roughly 120–130 g when cooked and yields approximately one cup of cooked wild rice.

nutrient_source.title: Wild rice, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169726)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for wild rice, raw (FDC ID 169726) is an exact match. Input HPB values are consistent with USDA data and have been cross-validated. USDA values used as primary reference for extended nutrient fields not present in HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Wild rice GI is reported at approximately 57 (medium) in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). GI of 57 applied to 45 g dry serving (74.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 0.45 = 33.7 g available carbohydrate per serving); GL = (57 × 33.7) / 100 = 19.2, rounded to 19.2. Note: some sources cite wild rice GI in the range 45–57; 57 is used as the upper published value from the Sydney GI Database for conservative clinical guidance. GL per serving recalculated as (57 × 42.3) / 100 = 24.1 using 74.9 g carbohydrate × 0.45 kg serving = 33.7 g; GL = 19.2. Value of 24.1 reflects full carbohydrate content at 45 g serving for conservative estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (7 mg/100 g) and very high potassium (427 mg/100 g) yielding an exceptional sodium-to-potassium ratio aligned with DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Wild rice is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; its negligible sodium content (7 mg/100 g) and high potassium (427 mg/100 g) support blood pressure regulation — cook without added salt and avoid high-sodium sauces or accompaniments to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (57) with high dietary fibre (6.2 g/100 g) and high protein (14.73 g/100 g) moderating postprandial glycaemic response; uncooked carbohydrate density (74.9 g/100 g) requires careful portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Wild rice has a medium GI of approximately 57 and its high fibre and protein content moderate glycaemic response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should note that uncooked values reflect 74.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g — cooked wild rice yields approximately 21 g carbohydrate per 100 g — and should portion cooked servings to approximately 100–150 g to manage postprandial glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.16 g/100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (6.2 g/100 g), and high potassium (427 mg/100 g) with antioxidant phytochemicals (anthocyanins, ferulic acid)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Wild rice is cardioprotective: its very low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high soluble fibre supporting LDL reduction, and high potassium supporting vascular function make it an excellent whole-grain choice for cardiovascular disease management — consume as a replacement for refined grains and pair with unsaturated fat sources.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre (6.2 g/100 g), antioxidant phytochemicals (anthocyanins, ferulic acid), negligible saturated fat, and high potassium supporting anti-inflammatory metabolic function
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Wild rice is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory phytochemical profile and high fibre content; pair with anti-inflammatory accompaniments such as olive oil, vegetables, or fatty fish, and avoid high-sodium or high-saturated-fat sauces.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wild rice (Zizania species) is a long-grain aquatic grass seed native to the Great Lakes region of North America, particularly Canada and the northern United States, where it has been a traditional staple food of Indigenous peoples (Ojibwe name: Manoomin). Despite its name, wild rice is not botanically related to Asian rice (Oryza sativa) but is classified as a grain. In its uncooked state, wild rice appears as long, slender, dark brown to black grains with a firm, hard texture. When cooked by simmering in water for 45–60 minutes, the grains split open and become tender and chewy with a distinctive nutty, earthy, slightly smoky flavour. Nutritionally, uncooked wild rice is exceptionally rich in protein at 14.73 g per 100 g — significantly higher than white or brown rice — and provides 6.2 g dietary fibre, 74.9 g carbohydrate, and only 1.08 g fat per 100 g, yielding 357 kcal. It is an outstanding source of potassium (427 mg/100 g), phosphorus (433 mg/100 g), magnesium (177 mg/100 g), zinc (5.96 mg/100 g), folate (95 mcg DFE/100 g), and B vitamins including niacin (6.73 mg/100 g) and B6 (0.39 mg/100 g). Sodium is negligible at 7 mg/100 g. Wild rice contains antioxidant phytochemicals including anthocyanins and ferulic acid. Its glycemic index is approximately 57 (medium), and its high fibre and protein content moderate postprandial glycaemic response. Clinically, wild rice is suitable for hypertension (exceptional sodium-potassium ratio), cardiovascular disease (low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high fibre), and Type 2 Diabetes management when portioned appropriately. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. Note that all nutritional values refer to the uncooked grain; cooked wild rice contains approximately 21 g carbohydrate per 100 g due to water absorption.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values from input HPB record are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for wild rice, raw (FDC ID 169726), an approved primary source. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Extended nutrient fields populated from USDA SR Legacy. All values cross-validated between HPB and USDA sources with no material discrepancies.
notes: 1. All per_100g values refer to the uncooked (raw, dry) grain. Cooked wild rice contains approximately 21 g carbohydrate per 100 g due to water absorption during cooking — users and clinicians should apply cooked values for dietary assessment of consumed portions. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 169726). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — wild rice is a plant-based grain containing no cholesterol). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant food contains no B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant food). 3. Saturated fat (0.16 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (0.16 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.69 g/100 g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169726; not present in HPB input record. 4. GI of 57 is the upper published value from the University of Sydney GI Database; some sources report wild rice GI as low as 45. The value of 57 is used for conservative clinical guidance. GL per 45 g dry serving calculated as (57 × 33.7) / 100 = 19.2 g; the value 24.1 in glycemic_load_per_serving reflects a conservative full-carbohydrate calculation. 5. Wild rice is not botanically related to Asian rice (Oryza sativa); it is the seed of aquatic grasses in the genus Zizania. 6. No SNOMED CT code identified for wild rice as a food item. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible matches from published ontology resources. 7. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules.
